title: Vocational education as the risky choice: narratives of educational choice among students with immigrant background
abstract: Although vocational education offers viable pathways to employment, research has yet to understand why students with immigrant backgrounds tend to avoid vocational tracks. This longitudinal qualitative study explores how students perceive and talk about their upper secondary educational choices, including interviewees both with and without immigrant backgrounds. The article shows how the students draw on a shared narrative where vocational education is constructed as the 'risky choice' - characterised as easy, unidimensional, and limiting future opportunities. For students with immigrant backgrounds, the narrative serves two distinct purposes: maintaining educational mobility aspirations while simultaneously avoiding stereotypical perceptions of high-achieving immigrant students. Conversely, students with Norwegian-born parents use the same narrative to legitimise their postponing of future educational choices. Together, the study illustrates how seemingly identical educational choice narratives are used for different purposes across groups, exposing how individuals are embedded in different cultural contexts in which their choices become meaningful.
journal: British Journal of Sociology of Education
